topic: My bank has charged me nineteen dollars and forty two cents a month for eleven years from a coffee shop on Pearl Street and Pearl Street is a parking lot the city paved over in two thousand seven
title: My Bank Charged Me Nineteen Dollars a Month for Eleven Years From a Coffee Shop on a Street That No Longer Exists. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I was pulling eleven years of bank statements for a mortgage refinance when I noticed a recurring monthly charge of nineteen dollars and forty two cents from a place called Aspen Coffee at four twelve Pearl Street. Pearl Street was paved over and turned into the county courthouse parking lot in two thousand seven. I have never bought coffee there. The bank says the merchant has been active and processing my card every month for eleven straight years.
description:
I was pulling eleven years of bank statements for a mortgage refinance when I noticed a recurring monthly charge of nineteen dollars and forty two cents from a place called Aspen Coffee at four twelve Pearl Street. Pearl Street was paved over and turned into the county courthouse parking lot in two thousand seven. I have never bought coffee there. The bank says the merchant has been active and processing my card every month for eleven straight years.

The charges had been quietly buried in my monthly autopay summary the entire time. The amounts varied by a few cents and looked like any other small recurring bill. The bank confirmed the merchant is a real Visa merchant with a federal tax ID that was last filed in two thousand six. The historical society confirmed Aspen Coffee operated at four twelve Pearl from nineteen ninety eight until the block was demolished. The owner died in two thousand eleven. The bank still has my signed receipt from last Tuesday.

There is a detail I did not understand until I walked back to the lot at dusk on the day before my refinance closed. There was a cardboard sign zip tied to the parking lot light pole. The sign said the coffee shop was closing forever on Saturday. The next monthly statement arrived three days later. The line item had a new name on it. Watch to the end.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I was pulling eleven years of bank statements off the online portal to send to my mortgage broker for a refinance. The broker had asked for everything since the original loan closed. I had the statements open in a single long scroll on my laptop and I was tabbing through them while I drank coffee at the kitchen table. About halfway through the third year I noticed a recurring line item. Nineteen dollars and forty two cents. The merchant name was Aspen Coffee. The address line read four twelve Pearl Street. The same line item appeared in the month before it. And the month before that. I scrolled backward all the way to the first statement. Eleven years. Every single month. Aspen Coffee, four twelve Pearl Street. Pearl Street was paved over and turned into the courthouse parking lot in two thousand seven.

close shot of a mug of black coffee beside an open laptop on a kitchen table, only the rim of the mug and the edge of the keyboard visible, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a paper bank statement with the address line four twelve Pearl Street typed in small print, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: Our downtown is six blocks. The courthouse takes up the center block. There is a hardware store on the south corner, a diner on the north corner, and a small bookshop two doors down from the diner. The streets are laid out in a simple grid. Main Street runs north to south. Pearl Street used to run east to west along the south side of the courthouse. Pearl Street is not there anymore. In two thousand seven the city bought the entire block of small storefronts on Pearl, demolished them, and paved the lot for courthouse overflow parking. I was in high school when it happened. I remember the dust on the cars. I have lived in this town my entire life. I buy my coffee at the diner on Main. I have never bought coffee anywhere else.

sections.narration: I do not read my bank statements closely. I check the total at the bottom of each month and I look at the column of incoming deposits against the column of outgoing autopays. If the totals line up within a few dollars I move on. I only read the line items in detail at tax time, and even then I only flag items above twenty dollars for my accountant. The Aspen Coffee charge fell just under that threshold every single month. The amount also varied by a few cents from month to month. One month it was nineteen dollars and thirty seven cents. The next month it was nineteen dollars and forty five cents. The average was nineteen forty two. The variation made the line look like a tip adjusted purchase from a real cafe. It never tripped any alert. It never caught my eye.

sections.narration: On my lunch break I walked the four blocks from my office to the courthouse parking lot. The walk took eight minutes. The lot was full of cars. I stood on the southwest corner where four twelve Pearl would have been if Pearl still existed. The asphalt was the same black as the rest of the lot. The yellow painted parking lines ran straight across the spot. There was no sign. There was no cornerstone. There was no plaque. There was nothing to mark that a coffee shop had ever stood there. I walked the whole perimeter to be sure. I checked the city business directory on my phone while I stood there. There was no listing for Aspen Coffee. The directory had records back to two thousand seven. There was no Aspen Coffee in any of them.

sections.narration: I walked back to my office and told myself the simple answer. The charge had to be a legacy biller code. Banks process payments through merchant identifiers that sometimes outlive the businesses they are tied to. A bank fee or a subscription that I had set up a decade ago could easily have ended up mislabeled with an old merchant name and an old address. It happens. I had read about it in a personal finance article years ago. I told myself I would dispute the charge with the bank after the refinance closed. The refinance was set to close on Friday. I did not want to open a fraud investigation in the middle of the underwriting process. It would tie up the file. The eleven year pattern could wait two more weeks. I went back to work and stopped thinking about it.

sections.narration: I called the bank the next morning before work. The representative pulled up the merchant record. She told me the charge was not a legacy code. The charge was being processed through a live Visa merchant identifier. The merchant name on file was Aspen Coffee. The address on file was four twelve Pearl Street. The merchant category code was eating places and restaurants. The merchant had a registered federal tax identification number. The tax identification number had last been filed with the federal government in two thousand six. She told me the merchant had processed a charge against my card every single month for one hundred and thirty two consecutive months. There had not been a single skipped or declined transaction in eleven years. She asked me if I wanted to file a dispute. I asked her to hold the file.

sections.narration: After work I drove to the historical society on the north end of town. The society keeps the city's old records in a converted house behind the library. The volunteer at the front desk pulled the city directory binders for me. Pearl Street existed from eighteen ninety four to two thousand seven. Four twelve Pearl had housed a series of small storefronts over the decades. From nineteen ninety eight until the block was demolished it had been Aspen Coffee. The owner of record was Howard Ehlers. The volunteer remembered him. She told me he closed the shop the week before the city took the block. She told me he moved into the county nursing home in two thousand eight and died there in two thousand eleven. She showed me a photograph of the storefront. The awning was a soft cream color.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a small historical society house at dusk with a hand painted wooden sign on the front lawn, single sodium light, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: On Wednesday morning the bank emailed me a digitized receipt for one of the Aspen Coffee charges. They said it would help me decide whether to pursue the dispute. I opened the attachment on my laptop. The receipt was printed on thermal paper. The header read Aspen Coffee, four twelve Pearl Street. The line items were a twelve ounce drip coffee for two dollars and seventy five cents and a slice of banana bread for four dollars and fifty cents. The subtotal was seven twenty five. The tax was sixty seven cents. The tip line was filled in by hand for eleven dollars and fifty cents. The total at the bottom was nineteen dollars and forty two cents. The signature on the bottom of the receipt was mine. The date stamp printed at the top of the receipt was the previous Tuesday.

sections.narration: I drove back to the courthouse parking lot at dusk on Thursday. The lot was empty by the time I got there. The sodium light over the southwest corner had just clicked on. I walked to the spot where four twelve Pearl would have been. The asphalt looked the same as it had on Monday. Then I saw the sign. A small cardboard sign was zip tied to the base of the light pole. The sign was hand lettered in black marker. It read Aspen Coffee. Open since nineteen ninety eight. Closing forever Saturday. Thank you for eleven good years. Saturday was the day after the refinance was set to close. The cardboard was the same soft cream color as the awning in the historical society photograph. I knelt down beside the pole and read the sign three times.

sections.narration: The refinance closed on Friday afternoon. The papers were signed. The new loan funded. On Saturday at noon I drove back to the parking lot. The lot was empty. The sign was gone. The zip tie was gone. There was no mark on the pole where it had been. The next monthly bank statement arrived three days later. I scrolled through it on my laptop at the kitchen table. The nineteen dollars and forty two cents charge from Aspen Coffee did not appear. In its place a new recurring charge had been added. Nineteen dollars and forty two cents. The merchant name was Maple Bookshop. The address line read four twelve Pearl Street. I called the historical society. They have no record of a Maple Bookshop at that address. I called the bank. They told me the merchant has been active for eleven years.
